title: Obesogen Holdover: Prenatal Exposure Predicts Cardiometabolic Risk Factors in Childhood.
ehr_evidence: The study used the Rhea mother-child cohort, which involved direct blood samples and physical measurements, rather than EHR or linked health data.
summary: A study investigating the link between prenatal exposure to persistent organic pollutants (POPs) and cardiometabolic risk factors in children aged 4 in Greece.
key_findings: A 10-fold increase in maternal HCB concentrations was associated with higher risks of generalized and abdominal obesity, greater skinfold thickness, higher systolic blood pressure, and a modest increase in child BMI z-score.
Prenatal DDE exposure was associated with higher BMI z-score, increased risk of abdominal obesity, and higher diastolic blood pressure.
PCBs were not associated with any of the risk factors assessed.
captured_features: maternal blood concentrations of DDE, HCB, and PCBs
population: Nearly 700 mother-child pairs from the Rhea cohort in Greece
limitations: Unclear implications for future health outcomes
